debate.evidence: Vercel's own /docs uses Next.js and ships ~120kb JS per static page (measured via WebPageTest). Astro equivalents I've measured ship 20-40kb per page including ONE interactive island.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: That JS measurement gap is real but it's compressing two things: (1) framework overhead, (2) shared components. The Nav component is the same in Next.js or Astro - if it's interactive, it ships. Astro's win is when 80%+ of the page is static. For our site, the docs sidebar is interactive (collapsible sections, search), the top nav has user state, and the demos are React-heavy. Astro's 'pure HTML' advantage doesn't apply once interactivity creeps in.
debate.evidence: Astro itself ships React runtime per page that has React islands. The 'zero JS' advantage requires careful authoring to maintain - one careless `<Component client:load />` and you're shipping React anyway.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Careful authoring is the whole point of Astro - it makes JS shipping VISIBLE and OPT-IN. In Next.js, JS shipping is implicit and hard to audit. Astro's directives (`client:load`, `client:visible`, `client:idle`) let you pick exactly when to hydrate, including 'never' for static demos that look interactive but aren't. That fine-grained control is a feature, not friction.
debate.evidence: Astro's client directives let you ship interactivity that doesn't run until viewport intersection. Next.js client components hydrate eagerly by default; lazy hydration requires explicit dynamic imports.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Real win for Astro on hydration control. Where Astro loses: state across islands. In a docs site with a 'theme toggle' that affects all islands, you have to either (a) use a shared store (Nano Stores in Astro docs) which means each island reads from a global, or (b) accept that islands are isolated. Next.js with a single Providers tree gives shared context naturally.
debate.evidence: I've built an Astro site with 30+ islands per page sharing user-state. The Nano Stores integration works but the mental model of 'islands subscribe to a global store' is harder to reason about than React's tree.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Shared state across islands is genuinely awkward in Astro. But for a docs/marketing site, how much shared client state IS there? Theme toggle, that's basically it. CSS variables handle theme without any cross-island state. Auth state on the 10% authenticated pages can be a full React app within those routes - Astro supports nested layouts that are 'fully React' inside the React subtree.
debate.evidence: Astro's hybrid mode lets you use a full SSR React layout for authenticated routes while keeping marketing/docs as islands. Best of both.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: That hybrid model is interesting. Pushback: build complexity. Two rendering models (Astro static + React SSR for auth) means two mental models for the team to maintain. With Next.js, there's one model (App Router) for all routes. The DX consistency matters when 4 engineers are touching different parts of the site.
debate.evidence: Onboarding cost at Vercel for App Router is roughly 1 week to productive. Astro + hybrid is 2-3 weeks because engineers learn both Astro's syntax and the React subtree pattern.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Onboarding cost is real. But Next.js App Router has its own complexity - RSC vs client components, the 'use client' boundary, Server Actions, route caching gotchas, Middleware. None of that is needed for marketing/docs. Astro is dramatically simpler for what marketing/docs ACTUALLY needs. Where Next.js wins: the authenticated 10% benefit from RSC's data fetching and Server Actions for forms.
debate.evidence: Astro's mental model for static content is 'this is HTML, with some islands' - shorter ramp for designers and content engineers contributing pages.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: For this team and product mix: Astro for marketing/docs (the 90%) with React islands for demos, separate Next.js subdomain (app.example.com) for authenticated. Treat them as two products. Marketing perf wins via Astro; authenticated UX wins via Next.js features. Slight build/deploy complexity but each tool is best-in-class for its surface.
debate.evidence: This is the architecture at Resend, Cal.com, and several other dev-tool companies. Marketing and app are separate deployments.

where_they_agree: Marketing/docs CWV is sales-impactful and must be optimized
Astro ships less JS for static-heavy content
Authenticated/interactive surfaces benefit from full React framework features
Splitting into marketing + app subdomains is a viable architecture
MDX + custom components is supported well in both frameworks
where_they_disagree: Whether one-framework consistency outweighs the right-tool-per-surface argument
How much shared client state typical sites actually need
Whether Astro's hybrid model adequately handles authenticated subtrees
context_dependent_answer: For this product (60% static, 30% demo, 10% auth): Astro for marketing/docs/demos, separate Next.js for authenticated app. For a team that requires a single framework: Next.js App Router with disciplined client component boundaries. For a 100% static blog: Astro, no question. For an interactive-heavy SaaS dashboard: Next.js or Remix, not Astro.

strengths: Static content ships as HTML - zero React runtime cost
weaknesses: Two mental models if some routes are full React
Shared state across islands requires Nano Stores or similar
Astro-specific syntax (.astro files) for engineers to learn

decision_framework: debate-035: judge the build tool choices debate in this scenario: A team is building a content-heavy marketing + docs site with embedded interactive demos (60% static pages, 30% interactive demos, 10% authenticated). Choosing between Next.js App . Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors choose frameworks by popularity or familiarity. Seniors recognize different surfaces have different rendering needs and that 'one framework' is not always the optimum. A marketing site shipping React runtime to every visitor is shipping unnecessary bytes to its highest-value audience (prospects evaluating CWV). The right answer is often two frameworks deployed separately. Also missed: 'static' is a misnomer - modern static frameworks support per-request data fetching, just without client-side JS. Astro is not 'old SSG' - it's a hybrid you can grow into.

scenario: A 30-engineer org is deciding: build internal components from scratch on Radix primitives, adopt shadcn/ui (copy-paste components), or pull in Mantine as a dependency. Current state: three product teams each have their own ad-hoc component set, drift is causing design QA pain.
context: 6-month window to consolidate. Designers want pixel control. New hires need ramp-up under 2 weeks. Bundle budget is 200KB initial JS.
alex_initial_position: shadcn/ui. You own the source code, you can modify aggressively, no version-lock pain, no transitive dep bloat. The 'copy-paste' criticism is backwards - the components ARE yours, which is what an internal library is supposed to be. Radix underneath gives you accessibility for free.
sam_initial_position: Mantine. Stop reinventing wheels. Mantine has 100+ tested components with accessibility audited. You spend the next 6 months building Buttons and Selects, or you spend them shipping features. Yes there's bundle cost, but tree-shaking + targeted imports gets you there.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Mantine's theming model fights you the moment your designer wants something opinionated. Their Button has 5 variants, your designer wants 7, and now you're either wrapping every Mantine component or fighting their CSS-in-JS for overrides. With shadcn, the Button IS yours - 7 variants means editing one file.
debate.evidence: Last role tried Mantine for an enterprise app. Within 4 months we had a 'components-overrides' folder with 40 wrapper components. The 'free' library was now a layer we maintained on top of.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: You're describing tooling fit, not library quality. Same problem hits shadcn the second your design diverges from shadcn's defaults - you fork the source, and now your 'copy-paste' has a fork date stamped on it. In 18 months when Radix v2 ships with a behavior fix, your forked component doesn't get it. With Mantine, the upgrade is a version bump.
debate.evidence: Forks have a half-life. They feel free at week 1 and feel like technical debt at month 12.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Mantine's version bumps have broken our code three times in two years. Their 6.x to 7.x migration was a week per team. The 'upgrade is a version bump' framing only holds when the library author is conservative. Mantine is not conservative - they iterate on API. shadcn doesn't have that problem because there's no central version to bump.
debate.evidence: Mantine 7 rewrote the styling layer entirely. We had to rewrite every custom component.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: OK conceding Mantine's churn. But shadcn has the opposite problem: zero coordination. Three teams 'own' three forks of the same Button. Drift returns. The whole point of consolidation is preventing that.
debate.evidence: shadcn-style adoption at a previous company resulted in 4 versions of the Modal in 18 months.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: That's a governance failure not a tool failure. The fix: a single 'components-internal' package, only one team owns it, others contribute via PR. shadcn becomes the starting template, not the everyone-copy-paste pattern.
debate.evidence: This is how Linear's internal component lib works. shadcn as scaffolding, single owner team, PR process for changes.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: That works if you have a platform team to staff it. 30 engineers, you might have 2 on a platform team. Maintaining 100 components from scratch is a job. Mantine pays that maintenance for you.
debate.evidence: Component maintenance is a real cost: a11y fixes, browser bug workarounds, edge cases.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: You don't maintain 100 components. You maintain the 30 you actually use. shadcn registry has maybe 50 components - copy the 30 you need, leave the rest. Maintenance scope is bounded by usage.
debate.evidence: YAGNI for component libraries. Most apps use 30 core components plus a long tail of one-offs.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Fair. Compromise: shadcn for the 30 core (Button, Input, Select, Dialog, etc.), but use Radix primitives directly for the long tail rather than forking shadcn versions. That keeps fork surface small.
debate.evidence: This split has worked at Vercel - shadcn for common, raw Radix for niche.

where_they_agree: Three teams forking the same Modal is the failure to avoid
A single platform team should own internal components
Radix primitives are the right accessibility foundation
Bundle budget rules out runtime-CSS-in-JS heavy libraries
where_they_disagree: Whether maintenance burden of 30 owned components > maintenance burden of upgrading a library
Whether forking diverges over time (Sam: yes) or stays bounded with governance (Alex)
Whether 'pixel control' is worth the maintenance cost
context_dependent_answer: 30-eng org with 6mo consolidation window: shadcn + Radix + single platform team owner. For a 5-eng startup: Mantine, ship faster. For a 200-eng org with strict design governance: build from Radix, full custom (you can afford the team). External-facing component lib (open source): copy shadcn's distribution model.

strengths: Source is yours - any change is a local PR
weaknesses: You maintain it including bug fixes
Long-tail components require building from Radix yourself
No community for your version of the component
Mantine's API surface in your dependency tree
Theme overrides fight you for non-default designs
Major-version migrations are work
Bundle cost even with tree-shaking

decision_framework: debate-036: judge the component library strategy debate in this scenario: A 30-engineer org is deciding: build internal components from scratch on Radix primitives, adopt shadcn/ui (copy-paste components), or pull in Mantine as a dependency. Current stat. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors compare libraries on feature checkboxes. The senior insight: component library choice is a question about who owns the maintenance burden over multi-year horizons. shadcn moves the burden to YOUR team. Mantine moves it to the LIBRARY team but introduces upgrade-coupling risk. Both costs are real; they just hit at different times. Also missed: the 'fork' is not a one-time event - it's a continuous decision to not upstream changes. That has compounding cost.

scenario: A SaaS dashboard with 40+ widgets per page (charts, tables, KPIs) needs an error boundary strategy. One widget failing shouldn't crash the page, but the team disagrees: per-widget granular boundaries, route-level boundaries with a per-widget Suspense+fallback, or top-level boundary with telemetry-driven recovery.
context: Pages composed dynamically based on user dashboards. Average page: 25 widgets, max 60. Error rate: ~0.05% of widget renders fail (data shape mismatch from API drift mostly). React 19 is available; team is considering Activity for hidden-state recovery.
alex_initial_position: Per-widget boundaries. The error rate is 0.05% per widget × 25 widgets = ~1.2% page-level corruption rate if any error kills the page. Granular boundaries cap blast radius. Telemetry + UI for 'this widget failed, retry' is the right user story. Cost: 25 ErrorBoundary components per render. Negligible.
sam_initial_position: One boundary per logical 'section', not per widget. 25 boundaries per page means 25 fallback components, 25 retry handlers, 25 telemetry call sites. That's surface area you have to maintain. Group widgets into ~4-6 logical sections; one boundary per section. Same blast-radius story for the user, less code to maintain.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Section-level boundaries mean one bad widget takes down 5 widgets in that section. Telemetry shows the bad one, but the user sees a 'section unavailable' state for the other 4 working widgets. That's worse UX for marginal code savings.
debate.evidence: We measured this at Linear's inbox - per-card boundaries had 2.3% degraded states vs 11% with grouped boundaries. Same underlying error rate.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Your numbers are real but you're describing inbox cards which are independent units. Dashboard widgets often correlate - 'recent sales' chart and 'revenue summary' KPI may both fail because they share an upstream data source. Per-widget boundaries show 2 fallback states for one underlying failure, which looks worse than one section fallback that says 'sales data unavailable'.
debate.evidence: User research at Stripe Dashboard: users prefer one 'section unavailable' to multiple individual 'failed' badges for correlated failures. Cognitive load.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: That's solved with a shared fallback design: per-widget boundary, but when multiple widgets in the same data domain fail, fold the fallbacks into one. That requires per-widget boundaries first, then a coalescing UI layer. Not section boundaries.
debate.evidence: This is how Notion does inline failures: per-block boundaries, coalesced UI when failures are correlated.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Now you're describing a custom error-tracking layer on top of error boundaries. Not 'just per-widget boundaries' - it's per-widget boundaries plus a coalescing layer with error correlation. The maintenance cost I was avoiding is now real. We're agreeing the simple per-widget approach isn't enough; we just disagree on how to evolve from there.
debate.evidence: The 'add a coalescing layer' is exactly the section-level grouping, but late-bound at render time.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: OK granted - 'per-widget plus coalescing' is a more honest description. But the architectural shape differs: boundaries are at widget level (small, dumb fallbacks), with a higher-level reporting layer for telemetry/coalescing. With section boundaries, the boundary IS the coalescer and you lose granularity. The architectural shape matters for future flexibility - tomorrow we want to retry individual widgets, with section boundaries we can't.
debate.evidence: Granular retry: per-widget boundaries support 'this one retry' button. Section boundaries can only retry the whole section.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Fair point on retry. But React 19 Activity lets you stash the failed widget's state and retry without re-mounting siblings - within a section boundary. The lower granularity boundary doesn't preclude per-widget retry; it just changes the recovery mechanism.
debate.evidence: Activity allows preserving state across mount/unmount - retry via re-mounting the failed subtree only.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Activity is new enough that I wouldn't bet a production strategy on it being stable. But agreed it changes the math. For now: per-widget boundaries, coalescing UI for correlated failures, telemetry. Revisit when Activity is stable in 18 months.
debate.evidence: Conservative on new React APIs in load-bearing infrastructure.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Agreed. One more nuance: the boundary itself should be a lightweight HOC, not a full React component class. Otherwise 25 boundaries × component-level overhead adds up to non-trivial work per render.
debate.evidence: react-error-boundary's library overhead is ~3KB; multiplied by 25 instances per render it's still trivial, but the 'are we paying for this' question is fair.

where_they_agree: Top-level-only boundaries are wrong - whole page failure for one widget is unacceptable
Telemetry must accompany boundaries; without observability they're invisible
React 19 Activity may change the math in 12-18 months
Coalescing UI matters when failures correlate
where_they_disagree: Per-widget (Alex) vs per-section (Sam) as the default granularity
Whether the coalescing UI layer is enough complexity to push to section boundaries
How aggressively to bet on new React APIs for error recovery
context_dependent_answer: 40-widget dashboard with 0.05% error rate: per-widget boundaries + coalescing fallback UI + telemetry. For a 5-widget page: section-level is fine. For a single-page form (no widgets): one top-level boundary with retry. For mission-critical (e.g., medical dashboards): per-widget boundaries + circuit breakers (stop retrying after N failures) + escalation telemetry.

weaknesses: 25 boundary instances per page render
Coalescing must be a separate concern
Without coalescing, correlated failures look fragmented

code_examples.approach: Sam's section boundary with shared fallback
weaknesses: One bad widget hides working widgets
Section retry is heavyweight
Can't surface individual widget health

decision_framework: debate-037: judge the error boundary strategy debate in this scenario: A SaaS dashboard with 40+ widgets per page (charts, tables, KPIs) needs an error boundary strategy. One widget failing shouldn't crash the page, but the team disagrees: per-widget . Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors put one ErrorBoundary at the App root and call it done. The senior insight: error boundaries are a UX design question wearing technical clothes. Granularity dictates what 'partial failure' looks like to users. The right answer isn't 'how many boundaries' - it's 'what does a degraded state look like at what scope'. Also missed: error boundaries don't catch async errors, event handler errors, or errors during SSR - so they're necessary but never sufficient. You also need promise rejection handlers, async error reporting, and a strategy for SSR failure recovery.

scenario: A SaaS app's main bundle is 800KB gzipped, target is <250KB. Team debates: aggressive route-level splitting only, route + component-level for rarely-used features (modal libraries, charts), or feature-flag-driven dynamic imports (don't ship code for disabled features at all).
context: 24 routes total, 8 are high-traffic (cover 90% of sessions). Each route imports common deps (date picker, charts, rich editor) that aren't always used. Time-to-interactive on slow 4G is the KPI. Feature flag system already exists.
alex_initial_position: Route + component-level for known heavy deps. Charts and rich editors are 100KB+ each - splitting them at the component level (load when modal opens, when chart enters viewport) gets the biggest TTI wins. Feature flags adding another splitting layer is over-engineering until measured.
sam_initial_position: Route-level only as the discipline, then PROFILE before adding component-level. Component-level splitting has hidden costs: extra fetch on click, suspense fallback flashes, harder pre-fetching strategy. Most apps over-split and add user-perceived latency.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Profile-first sounds disciplined but it's how you ship an 800KB bundle. Heavy deps like @tiptap or recharts are 150KB+ each - you don't need a profiler to know they don't belong in the initial bundle. Default-split heavy known deps, profile the long tail.
debate.evidence: Bundle analyzer + lighthouse will identify the top 5 culprits in 10 minutes. Those are the wins. Profiling for the rest is correct.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Default-split heavy deps if they're behind interaction (modal, chart-on-scroll). But charts that render above the fold are part of the page - splitting them creates a suspense boundary in the user's primary viewport. You traded bundle size for visible loading state. That's not a win on TTI; it's a metric shuffle.
debate.evidence: Splitting an above-the-fold chart moved bundle from 600KB to 500KB but FCP went up 800ms because the chart is now async. We reverted.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Agreed - splitting above-the-fold defeats the purpose. Component-level should be for click-triggered (modals, dropdowns containing rich editors) and viewport-triggered (charts below the fold via IntersectionObserver-driven import). Above-the-fold stays in the route bundle.
debate.evidence: Linear's editor: the rich-text component loads on focus, not on mount. Saves 80KB from initial.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: On focus is a great pattern. Click is also good. Viewport-triggered for below-the-fold I'm more cautious about - the IntersectionObserver fires, we start the fetch, but if scroll is fast the user reaches the chart before it loads and sees a skeleton. The original bundle would have rendered immediately.
debate.evidence: For scroll-driven dashboards, the 'savings' of below-the-fold splits don't materialize when users scroll faster than the fetch.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Fix is rootMargin: load 200px before the viewport. Plus prefetch on app idle (requestIdleCallback) so the chunk is in HTTP cache before scroll. Not a problem if the strategy includes prefetch.
debate.evidence: Vercel's dashboard does this - chunks prefetched on idle, IntersectionObserver as fallback.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Idle prefetch is correct but it adds complexity: you need an idle scheduler, a manifest of routes-to-chunks-to-prefetch, and logic to avoid prefetching on slow networks (saveData header, effective-type). At that point the splitting strategy IS a system, not just 'lazy()'. Worth doing? Yes. But it's not 'split aggressively'; it's 'split + orchestrate prefetch'.
debate.evidence: Next.js builds this orchestration in. Roll-your-own takes a week of work.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Granted. So: default to route-level for cold paths; component-level + idle prefetch for known heavy deps behind interaction; never split above-the-fold of high-traffic routes. Feature-flag-driven splitting is the layer to consider after this is working.
debate.evidence: Layered strategy with measurable wins per layer.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Feature-flag-driven splits I push back on hard. They couple your bundle graph to your flag state. Flag flips become 'load a chunk that wasn't in the prefetch manifest' = slow first interaction after enabling a feature. Better: ship the code, let the flag gate its UI. The bundle savings rarely outweigh the operational complexity.
debate.evidence: A previous role tried flag-driven splits. Every flag flip required cache busting and a new deploy. Reverting was painful.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Strong agree. Flag-driven splits are a 99th-percentile optimization. Default: ship the code, gate the UI. Only split by flag for truly massive features (10% of bundle for a feature 5% of users see).
debate.evidence: The pre-condition for flag-driven splits is 'feature is genuinely large and genuinely rare'.

where_they_agree: Above-the-fold splitting is a metric trap
Idle prefetch is required for non-trivial component-level splits
Feature-flag-driven splits are a last resort, not a default
Route-level splitting is the universal baseline
where_they_disagree: Whether default-split-heavy-deps or profile-first is the right starting point
How aggressive viewport-triggered splitting should be
context_dependent_answer: SaaS with 24 routes targeting <250KB: route-level baseline, component-level for known heavy interaction-triggered deps (modals, dropdowns), idle prefetch for likely-next interactions, above-the-fold stays in route bundle. Marketing site (high SEO value, low interactivity): aggressive route-level only, no component-level. Internal admin (low traffic, no perf budget): no splitting, simpler builds. Content site with embeds: split per-embed type with intersection-triggered loads.

strengths: Heavy deps not in initial bundle
Idle prefetch hides latency on slow networks (if respected)
weaknesses: Suspense fallback visible on slow networks
Prefetch manifest grows as features grow
saveData/4G heuristics need handling

strengths: Each route bundle is one fetch
No per-component suspense flashes within a route
weaknesses: Route bundles can grow large if route imports heavy deps used rarely
No way to skip code for unused features within a route

decision_framework: debate-038: judge the code splitting philosophy debate in this scenario: A SaaS app's main bundle is 800KB gzipped, target is <250KB. Team debates: aggressive route-level splitting only, route + component-level for rarely-used features (modal libraries,. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors split everything they can. The senior insight: code splitting is a redistribution of latency, not a removal. You take bytes off initial download and put them onto interaction-time fetch. That's a win IF the interaction has visual latency budget AND prefetch is in place. If either is missing, splitting makes UX worse. Also missed: the 'bundle size' metric is upstream of TTI but not the same thing. A 300KB bundle that renders immediately can beat a 200KB bundle with a Suspense flash on first paint.

scenario: A custom <DataGrid> with editable cells needs to support keyboard navigation, screen readers, and rapid power-user editing. Team debates: implement the ARIA grid pattern (full role=grid + aria-rowindex, etc.) vs. native <table> with custom cell editing on top vs. role=application escape hatch with custom keyboard handling.
context: Internal admin tool used by ~50 ops users who live in keyboard. ~30% of users use screen readers (compliance audit found this). Cells: text, dropdown, date picker. Up to 1000 rows visible (virtualized).
alex_initial_position: Native <table> + custom cell-editing layer. The ARIA grid pattern is complex and brittle - one missing aria-attribute cascades into broken screen reader experiences. Native table semantics get you 80% of accessibility for free; the editing UX is a thin layer on top.
sam_initial_position: Full ARIA grid pattern. Native <table> doesn't support cell-level focus management, which is the entire point of an editable grid. The 'thin layer' you describe ends up reimplementing the grid pattern poorly. ARIA grid is hard but documented; implement it once correctly.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Native table + roving tabindex gives you cell-level focus. tabindex=0 on the active cell, tabindex=-1 on others. Arrow keys move focus. That IS the grid pattern's interaction model, but on actual table semantics. Screen readers announce 'row 5 column 3 of 1000' from the table's structure, not from aria-rowindex.
debate.evidence: VoiceOver and NVDA both announce table positions from native row/cell structure. ARIA grid duplicates that with aria-rowindex/colindex; it's belt-and-suspenders only if your table is virtualized and DOM doesn't match logical rows.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: That last point is the kill condition. We're virtualized with up to 1000 rows but only ~30 in the DOM. Native table semantics announce '30 rows' because that's the DOM truth. ARIA grid with aria-rowindex/colindex/rowcount lets you state '1000 rows, currently at row 547'. For a 1000-row virtualized grid, native table is wrong about size.
debate.evidence: This is the canonical case for aria-grid: virtualized lists where DOM size != logical size. Without aria-rowcount, screen readers announce the wrong totals.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: OK, that's the lever. Granted - for virtualized 1000-row, native table announces wrong totals. So we need aria-rowcount, aria-rowindex on visible rows. But that doesn't mean full role=grid. You can have <table> + aria-rowcount + aria-rowindex on virtualized rows. It's a hybrid.
debate.evidence: VoiceOver respects aria-rowcount on a <table> even though it's role=table, not role=grid.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Hybrid works for static cells but role=grid's contract is 'cells are focusable and editable'. role=table's contract is 'cells are read-only data'. When cells are editable, screen reader users expect grid behavior (Enter to edit, Escape to exit edit mode, F2 to begin edit). Without role=grid, you're sending mixed signals: table semantics but grid interaction.
debate.evidence: WAI-ARIA spec: role=grid implies editable/interactive cells; role=table implies static data.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Mixed signals is a real concern but in practice screen reader users adapt to the interaction model the app presents. NVDA's 'table mode' lets users navigate cells with Ctrl+Alt+Arrow regardless of role. The 'screen readers expect grid behavior' is a spec-purity argument, not a user-research observation.
debate.evidence: User testing with NVDA + JAWS users on editable table-role components didn't show confusion - they used standard table navigation and entered edit mode on Enter.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Your user test was 5 users? 20? At what point did you test with users of different screen reader versions, ages, and proficiency? The spec exists because the long tail of accessibility tools is wide. Specs ensure baseline behavior across the long tail; user tests with a sample of N users miss the long tail by definition.
debate.evidence: Compliance audits cite WCAG ATAG specifically because user tests can't cover the long tail.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Fair point - I conceded too quickly to user-test data. For a tool with 30% screen reader users and a compliance requirement: implement the full grid pattern. It's more work but the compliance + long-tail argument wins.
debate.evidence: 30% SR usage in a 50-user tool means 15 humans depend on this working across their varied stacks.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Agreed. One more nuance: the full grid pattern is hard. We should not roll our own. Use react-aria's useGrid + useGridCell or react-aria-components Table. They've implemented the pattern with the long-tail edge cases. Building from scratch is where teams fail the audit despite knowing the spec.
debate.evidence: react-aria handles 17 different focus management edge cases that the spec mentions in passing.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Library-backed grid pattern it is. The only thing I'd add: for the cell editors themselves (date picker, dropdown), use the same library's primitives. Mixing react-aria grid with custom editors creates focus-management inconsistencies.
debate.evidence: Focus restoration on Escape from a dropdown back to the grid cell requires the grid and dropdown to share focus context.

where_they_agree: Virtualized 1000-row grids need aria-rowcount/rowindex; native table alone is incorrect
react-aria's grid primitives over hand-rolling
Cell editors must share focus context with the grid
Compliance + 30% SR usage tips toward spec adherence over heuristic
where_they_disagree: Whether native <table> + aria attributes is sufficient (Alex initially) vs full role=grid (Sam)
How much weight to give user testing vs spec compliance
Whether 'mixed signals' (table role + interactive cells) confuses users in practice
context_dependent_answer: Internal admin with 30% SR usage and compliance: full ARIA grid via react-aria. Public marketing site with read-only data table: native <table>, no ARIA grid. Spreadsheet-like product (editable grid as core feature): full grid pattern with custom rendering on top of react-aria primitives. Static report table with sortable headers: native <table> + aria-sort, no grid.

strengths: Native table semantics work in most screen readers
weaknesses: Editable cells with table role sends mixed signals
Some screen reader / browser combos may handle role=table cell editing inconsistently
Cell-level edit mode UX has to be reimplemented
Doesn't satisfy strict WCAG grid pattern compliance

strengths: Focus management across cells + editors works correctly
Long-tail SR support tested by maintainers
weaknesses: Library lock-in for grid primitives
Learning curve for react-aria's state model
Bundle cost (~20KB)
Custom rendering requires understanding the abstraction

decision_framework: debate-039: judge the accessibility tradeoffs debate in this scenario: A custom <DataGrid> with editable cells needs to support keyboard navigation, screen readers, and rapid power-user editing. Team debates: implement the ARIA grid pattern (full role. Separate claims, evidence, counterexamples, operational consequences, migration cost, and the failure mode each side handles poorly.
what_juniors_miss: Juniors think accessibility is 'add aria-labels and you're done'. The senior insight: accessibility roles are CONTRACTS with screen readers. Picking the wrong role (table for an editable grid) sets user expectations that the app then violates. The choice between native semantics and ARIA roles isn't a preference - it's tied to whether you can fulfill the contract. Also missed: virtualization fundamentally changes accessibility. The DOM doesn't reflect the logical structure, so aria-rowcount/aria-rowindex/aria-setsize/aria-posinset become required, not optional. And user testing isn't enough - the long tail of AT/browser/version combinations needs spec compliance to cover what's untestable.

scenario: A growing frontend codebase (40 engineers, 4 product domains) is restructuring. Team debates: domain-driven feature folders (src/features/billing, src/features/onboarding), layer-driven (src/components, src/hooks, src/api), or hexagonal (src/domain, src/application, src/infrastructure, src/ui).
context: Currently monolithic with everything in src/components, src/hooks, src/api. Onboarding takes 2 weeks; engineers report fear of touching unfamiliar code. Code search across 4 domains is slow. PR review time has grown.
alex_initial_position: Feature folders. The unit of change is a feature, not a layer. When billing changes, you want all billing code colocated - the form, the hooks, the API client, the types. Cross-domain imports are the smell signal. Hexagonal is over-engineered for a frontend codebase.
sam_initial_position: Hexagonal, but pragmatic. Pure feature folders create duplication when two features need similar primitives (date picker, currency input). Hexagonal lets domain logic stay testable, UI stay swappable, infrastructure (API clients) stay mockable. The investment pays off at 40-engineer scale.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Hexagonal architecture is solving a backend problem - separating business logic from infrastructure for testability and swappability. Frontend infrastructure (HTTP client, browser APIs) doesn't swap. You're paying the architectural cost without getting the benefit. Feature folders get you the colocation win.
debate.evidence: In 8 years I've never seen a frontend swap its HTTP client without also rewriting the rest. The 'swappable infrastructure' axis is theoretical.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: You're conflating 'swap implementation' with 'isolate for testing'. Hexagonal's value on frontend is testing domain logic without rendering. A billing calculation engine is a function - it should not import React. Feature folders mix the calculation, the form, the API client, the rendering. Tests for the calculation now have to spin up React or mock 4 things.
debate.evidence: At Stripe Dashboard the billing math was in a frontend module that did import React. Tests took 200ms each. Extracted to a pure domain module, tests dropped to 5ms each.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: That's a 'extract pure logic into pure functions' pattern, not hexagonal. You can do that inside a feature folder: features/billing/domain.ts (pure), features/billing/api.ts (HTTP), features/billing/components/. The feature folder still works; you just have an internal layer split. Hexagonal mandates top-level src/domain, src/infrastructure, etc. which scatters one feature across 4 top-level folders.
debate.evidence: This is what shadcn-style internal pattern recommends: features/<name>/{domain,api,components,hooks}.ts.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: OK on the 'pure logic split inside feature' pattern. The harder case: shared primitives. A currency input is used by billing, settings, and invoicing. With feature folders, where does it live? features/billing? features/_shared/? Cross-feature imports are the smell you mentioned. Hexagonal puts shared primitives in a 'ui' layer that everyone imports.
debate.evidence: Shared component placement is the classic 'shared kernel' problem from DDD.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: src/shared or src/ui for cross-feature primitives. Feature folders + a shared bucket is normal. The rule: a primitive moves to shared when 3+ features use it; until then it lives in the originating feature.
debate.evidence: Rule of three. Premature sharing creates worse abstractions than duplication.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Rule of three works until it doesn't. The 'shared' folder grows; soon it has 50 components and no organization. We're back where we started with src/components, just renamed. Without internal structure to shared (ui primitives vs ui patterns vs ui layouts), shared becomes the new dumping ground.
debate.evidence: I've seen src/shared accumulate to 200 files in 18 months at a previous role.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: Then structure src/shared. src/shared/ui (atoms - Button, Input), src/shared/patterns (Card, Form), src/shared/layouts (PageShell, Sidebar). The structure is feature folders + a structured shared bucket. That's an evolved feature-folder approach, not hexagonal.
debate.evidence: Linear, Vercel Dashboard, and Notion all use approximately this shape.

debate.speaker: sam
debate.argument: Granted - structured-shared + feature folders is the practical implementation. Where I'd push: name the layers explicitly even within features. features/billing/{domain,application,infrastructure,ui}/ with infrastructure being adapters around APIs. That brings hexagonal's clarity without scattering features across top-level folders.
debate.evidence: Per-feature hexagonal layering preserves colocation while enforcing internal separation.

debate.speaker: alex
debate.argument: For a 40-eng codebase that's a reasonable convention. For a 5-eng codebase it would be over-engineering. Document it as: 'features/<name>/, with internal split when complexity demands it.' Don't enforce it from day 1 - let it emerge per feature.
debate.evidence: Premature structure creates the same pain as no structure. Convention should follow actual complexity.

where_they_agree: Pure feature folders without internal structure create messy shared buckets
Structured src/shared with sub-organization is required
Pure logic should be extracted from components for testability
Per-feature internal layering (domain/application/infrastructure/ui) is reasonable at scale
where_they_disagree: Whether hexagonal as top-level structure is justified for frontend
Whether internal feature structure should be mandated from day 1 or emerge
context_dependent_answer: 40 engineers, 4 domains: feature folders with structured src/shared + per-feature internal split where complexity exists. 5-engineer startup: flat src/components + src/api, feature folders premature. 200-engineer monorepo: feature folders as packages with explicit hexagonal layering, package boundaries enforce isolation. Single-page tool (5-10 components): flat is fine, don't structure at all.
